time: Fri 4–10:30 PM | Sat 11 AM–10:30 PM | Sun 11 AM–4 PM
venue: St. Nicholas Greek Orthodox Church, Marietta
blurb: The Marietta Greek Festival is back for a full three-day run, and it's the kind of event that earns its own weekend on the calendar. Think lamb on the spit, loukoumades, live Greek music, traditional dancing, and a church courtyard that turns into one of the best outdoor parties in Cobb County all spring. It's not a chain restaurant theme night — it's the real thing, put on by a tight-knit community that's been doing this for decades. Friday night has that date-night energy. Saturday is peak everything. Sunday is perfect if you want it a little more relaxed. Tickets and hours are at mariettagreekfestival.com — go ahead and put all three days in your phone.
scoring_notes: Annual but genuinely anticipated — this is the kind of community event East Cobb residents mark on the calendar. Strong fit for the 40s-and-up crowd, both couples and families with older kids. Accessible price point, easy to make a night out of it. Happening this week (May 15–17), well within the newsletter's publication window.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Midweek Market at Perimeter Summit**
📅 Wednesday, May 27 | Perimeter Summit, Brookhaven
🎟️ Free | [Learn More](placeholder)

A monthly outdoor market lands at Perimeter Summit on May 27 with local art, handmade goods, curated vintage clothing, candles, woodwork, and more. It's walkable, it's free, and it's right in the neighborhood.

This is a good midweek reset — the kind of thing you drop into on a lunch break or after work and end up staying longer than you planned. Local vendors, open air, no agenda.

Mark May 27 now before it sneaks up on you.
scoring_notes: Lowest of the three picks. Monthly recurring markets have limited uniqueness, and the May 27 date falls outside the immediate publishing week. However, it is hyper-local (Perimeter Summit, Brookhaven), free, and relevant to the audience's lifestyle. Works best as a third pick to round out the newsletter if the other two events are featured prominently. The recurring nature slightly hurts its can't-miss score, but it's the most locally relevant free event in the candidate pool.

event_name: Dirt Roads and Footpaths — Coro Vocati Concert
venue: St. Catherine's Episcopal Church, East Cobb
blurb: If you've never heard of Coro Vocati, here's your introduction: they're an Atlanta chamber choral ensemble, and this Sunday they're performing right here in East Cobb at St. Catherine's Episcopal Church. The concert runs from 3 to 5 p.m. and it's called 'Dirt Roads and Footpaths' — which already tells you this isn't a stiff, formal affair. It's an afternoon of live choral music in a beautiful church space, the kind of thing that feels like a genuine discovery rather than something you found on a poster. Free up your Sunday afternoon and go.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for the educated, culturally engaged segment of the East Cobb audience — especially empty nesters looking for something other than a bar or a festival. Choral chamber music is niche but exactly right for a $118K median income, 65% college-educated neighborhood. One-time performance adds urgency. Happening this Sunday (May 3), so timing is tight but workable for a newsletter publishing this week.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Envision Perimeter 2026**
📅 Tuesday, May 5 | Evening | Perimeter Summit, Brookhaven
🎟️ Ticketed — 15% off with code ROUGHDRAFT | [Get Tickets](placeholder)

What's actually happening in Sandy Springs, Dunwoody, and Brookhaven? That's the question Envision Perimeter 2026 is built to answer. This is a one-night event focused on the future of the exact neighborhoods this newsletter covers — development, community, growth, what's coming and what's changing.

If you live or work in the Perimeter area, this is the room you want to be in. The people shaping what this corridor looks like in five years will be there. So will your neighbors who actually pay attention to this stuff.

It's a Tuesday night well spent. Grab your ticket and use code ROUGHDRAFT to knock 15% off.
scoring_notes: Near-perfect demographic fit for this newsletter. The audience is educated (70% college+), high-income, and invested in their community — exactly who attends civic/community planning events. Hyper-local to the Perimeter coverage area. One-night-only in 2026, so it has real urgency. The discount code adds a tangible reader benefit, which is a bonus for newsletter credibility. Slightly more adult/professional skew, which matches this audience well.

event_name: Springfest at Round Trip Brewing Co.
venue: Round Trip Brewing Co., East Cobb
blurb: Round Trip Brewing is throwing a Springfest and it sounds exactly like a good Saturday. German-inspired beers, food, craft activities, patio vibes, and live music in the evening — all of it right here in East Cobb. It's casual enough to show up with no plan, but there's enough going on that you'll stay longer than you expected. Grab someone worth sitting outside with, pick up a beer, and let the evening do the rest. No ticket required.
scoring_notes: Great fit for the adult and empty-nester segment of this audience — low barrier to entry, local, casual but fun. German beer and patio music on a May evening plays very well with a 42-median-age crowd. Uniqueness is the weakest score here (brewery events happen regularly), but the German theme and live music give it more character than a typical tap-room night. East Cobb location is a strong plus.

venue: Byers Theatre, Sandy Springs Performing Arts Center, Sandy Springs
blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Mean Girls – Southeast Premiere**
📅 Running through Sunday, May 17 | Various showtimes | Byers Theatre, Sandy Springs Performing Arts Center
🎟️ Tickets vary | [Get Tickets](placeholder)

City Springs Theatre Company is staging the Southeast premiere of Mean Girls — the full Broadway musical, right here at City Springs. This isn't a touring bus-and-truck production passing through town. It's your local company swinging for something big, and the Southeast premiere tag means you genuinely can't see this version of the show anywhere else in the region.

The musical goes harder than the movie. It's got the sharp writing, the iconic moments, and a full score that makes the whole thing hit differently live. Whether you saw the original film a hundred times or just like a night out that's actually fun, this one delivers.

Tickets are moving. Grab them before the run ends on May 17.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for a mixed adult audience — Mean Girls skews toward women 25–45 but has broad crossover appeal for couples and friend groups. At median $105K income, ticket prices at a performing arts center are not a barrier. The Southeast premiere designation makes it genuinely can't-miss: readers can't see this production anywhere else in the region. City Springs is right in the Perimeter coverage area, making it hyper-local. Better for adult/date-night skew than family, which aligns with this audience being more adult-leaning than East Cobb.

event_name: The Melting Pot Market: Spring Market
venue: The Lawn at Grandscape, The Colony
blurb:
Grandscape's lawn becomes a marketplace this Saturday as The Melting Pot Market brings more than 80 vendors together for its spring edition. Think local makers, small businesses, food, and the kind of browsing that's actually fun rather than obligatory.

It's at The Colony — right in the heart of the coverage area — and it's free to walk around. Great for a Mother's Day weekend outing if you want to find something a little more thoughtful than a last-minute gift card. Grab lunch from a food vendor, let the kids explore, and support some local businesses while you're at it.

No tickets needed. Just show up.
scoring_notes: Strong geographic fit — Grandscape in The Colony is squarely inside the coverage area and well-known to this readership. The 80-vendor count gives it more scale than a typical weekend market, and the multicultural, community-driven framing adds some distinctiveness. Mother's Day weekend timing helps the hook. Uniqueness is the weakest of the three picks since markets are recurring in format, but the spring edition framing and large vendor count elevate it. Demographic fit is solid across the middle-income suburban and college-adjacent younger reader segments.

event_name: Powder Springs Seafood and Sounds Festival
blurb:
If you missed the last five years of this one, now is a great time to fix that.

Powder Springs' Seafood and Sounds Festival is back May 15-17 for its sixth straight year. The first year drew over 10,000 people and it's only grown from there, which tells you pretty much everything you need to know. Seafood, live music, and a full weekend to work with.

It's not East Cobb, but it's close, and it's genuinely worth the drive. Put it on the calendar now before the weekend fills up.
scoring_notes: This event is next week (May 15-17), so it works well as an advance-notice pick for a newsletter publishing this week. The proven draw (10,000+ at its first year) signals broad appeal. Food and music festivals fit East Cobb's income and lifestyle profile well. Main knock is it's not in East Cobb proper and full pricing/details are unclear from the candidate summary. Included as third pick because the other two strong candidates (Garden Tour, Springfest) are both today only.

blurb:
Ten years ago, a small neighborhood decided to do something a little magical. This Saturday night, the Sandy Springs Lantern Parade celebrates its 10th anniversary, and it has grown into one of the most beloved community traditions in the area.

The idea is simple and the effect is stunning: hundreds of people walking through the neighborhood at dusk, handmade lanterns glowing, live music filling the air. It feels like something out of another country, and it happens right here.

The 10th anniversary makes this one worth showing up for. This is not a regular Saturday night, and it is not the kind of thing you can recreate on your couch. Bring the kids, bring a neighbor, or just come on your own. It is free, it is outside, and it is genuinely beautiful. Do not miss this one.
scoring_notes: The 10th anniversary makes this a milestone event, not just a recurring attraction. Free entry removes any barrier for this mixed-income audience. The lantern parade format appeals across age groups, fitting the mix of young families, young professionals, and empty nesters in the Perimeter coverage area. Outdoor, evening, community-rooted events index very well with educated, urban-adjacent demographics. The Sandy Springs location is squarely in the coverage zone.

blurb:
If your idea of a good weekend involves being outside, finding something unexpected, and grabbing food that is better than it has any right to be at an outdoor festival, the Dunwoody Art Festival is calling your name.

Happening all weekend at Dunwoody Village Parkway, the festival draws artists from across the region selling original paintings, sculpture, jewelry, photography, and more. Live music runs throughout the day. Food and drinks are never far away.

It is the kind of event that is easy to spend two hours at and somehow still be there at closing time. Free to attend, stroller-friendly, and walkable from half the neighborhood. Go Saturday, go Sunday, or do both.
scoring_notes: Art festivals skew well toward educated, higher-income audiences who buy original art and enjoy curated outdoor experiences. The Dunwoody location is core Perimeter territory. Slightly lower uniqueness score because art festivals are a recurring annual event rather than a once-in-a-decade milestone, but the weekend-long run and high demographic alignment make it a strong pick. Free admission means no barrier for any segment of the audience.

blurb:
If you've been looking for a reason to get the family outside this weekend, this is it. The Flower Mound Arts Festival is happening today at Heritage Park — free admission, no catch, just a great morning or afternoon out.

Local and regional artists fill the park with paintings, sculpture, photography, and more. It's the kind of event where the kids can wander, you can browse without a agenda, and everyone finds something worth stopping for. Plus it's outdoors in May, which in North Texas means you go now before summer shows up and ruins everything.

Free parking, free entry, and it runs all day — swing by whenever it works for you.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for the Flower Mound and Highland Village segments of the audience, which skew affluent and educated — exactly the demographic that turns out for arts festivals. Free admission removes any friction. Family-friendly format matches the strongly family-heavy audience skew. Uniqueness is annual but once-a-year still qualifies as a can't-miss for the local arts calendar. Slightly lower uniqueness score than Brew & Que because arts festivals are a common format, but the local setting and free price point make it highly practical for this readership.

event_name: Springfest at Round Trip Brewing Co.
time: Afternoon, with live music in the evening
venue: Round Trip Brewing Co., East Cobb
price: Free (food and drinks available for purchase)
blurb:
Spring finally feels like spring, and Round Trip Brewing is leaning all the way into it.

Springfest at Round Trip is a full afternoon outside with German-inspired beers, food, craft activities, and live music once the sun starts dropping. It's casual, it's local, and it's right here in East Cobb, so you have zero excuses to skip it. Show up early to snag a spot on the patio, stay for the music.

With Mother's Day tomorrow, this is also a solid move for the moms in your life who would much rather have a cold beer and live music than a crowded brunch reservation. Just saying.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's income level ($118K median) makes a beer-and-food event with no cover charge a very low-friction outing. The adult skew (median age 42, mix of empty nesters and parents of teens/college kids) maps well to a brewery festival. It's happening right in the neighborhood, which is a strong local-pride angle. The Mother's Day weekend hook adds editorial relevance.

blurb:
If you have ever walked through someone else's backyard and thought, I want my yard to look like that, today is your day.

The Cobb County Master Gardener Volunteers are opening up four private gardens for a self-guided tour this Saturday. You move at your own pace, stop as long as you want, and pick up ideas you can actually use at home. This is the 23rd year they've done it, which means the gardens on this list have been curated, not just suggested.

May is peak season in a Georgia garden, so the timing couldn't be better. Grab a friend, bring your phone for photos, and go find your next project.
scoring_notes: Near-perfect fit for East Cobb's 42-median-age, high-homeownership audience. Gardening skews strongly toward established homeowners with disposable time and income. The mix of empty nesters and families with older kids means a Saturday morning self-guided tour works well for couples or solo outings. It's annual but genuinely seasonal and local, happening today only.

event_name: Food That Rocks Sandy Springs at City Springs
blurb:
Mark this one on the calendar now so you do not forget it in three weeks.

Food That Rocks Sandy Springs is back at City Springs on Thursday, June 4, and this year the lineup includes 25-plus restaurants from across the neighborhood, all in one place, with live music going the whole night. It is Taste of Atlanta's annual nod to the Sandy Springs dining scene, and the Sandy Springs dining scene has gotten very good.

Think of it as the most efficient way to try five restaurants you have been meaning to visit, without the wait or the separate checks. Outdoor, walkable, and built for a weeknight out with someone worth spending a Thursday evening with. Tickets are not out yet, so bookmark it now and move fast when they drop.
scoring_notes: Food-and-music festival format is a strong fit for the Perimeter audience: educated, higher-income, food-curious adults who go out regularly. City Springs is directly in the coverage zone. The June 4 date means this is a forward-looking feature rather than this-weekend urgency, which is a trade-off, but it gives readers lead time to plan. Lower uniqueness score because food festivals recur annually, but the specific Sandy Springs culinary angle adds local relevance. Worth including as a 'coming up' pick.

blurb:
Dust off your boots and head to the lake. Little Elm's Brew & Que brings legendary BBQ, craft drinks, and live music together right on the shores of Lewisville Lake at Little Elm Park — and this one is your backyard.

It's a full Texas throwdown: competition BBQ, cold craft beers, and live music with a crowd that knows how to have a good time outdoors. The kind of event where the kids can run around while the adults eat something worth talking about later.

Tickets are available through 99.5 The Wolf — head there now and grab yours before they're gone.
scoring_notes: This is a direct bullseye for the Lewisville Lake audience. It's on the lake, it's Texas-flavored, it fits both the family-heavy and lake-lifestyle segments of the readership. BBQ and craft beer skew well for the 36-median-age crowd. Strong uniqueness as an annual signature event for the community — not something that happens every weekend. Price point is low relative to the $95K median income. High audience match across the mixed-income suburban and lake-lifestyle reader base.

venue: Holy Transfiguration Greek Orthodox Church, Marietta
blurb: The Marietta Greek Festival is back for its 36th year, and it's basically in your backyard. This isn't a new event trying to find its footing — it's a full-blown, multi-decade tradition with homemade Greek food, live music, and traditional dancing that people in East Cobb come back to every single year. Spanakopita, baklava, gyros, the works. The dancing alone is worth the trip. It runs Thursday through Saturday at Holy Transfiguration Greek Orthodox Church in Marietta, so you've got three chances to get there. Go hungry.
scoring_notes: Annual event but deeply local and beloved — East Cobb readers are the core audience for this. 36-year track record signals genuine community institution. Fits established families and empty nesters equally well. Free admission lowers the barrier; food spend is the real draw. Very close proximity to East Cobb boosts relevance. Docked on uniqueness since it recurs annually, but it only comes once a year and the community identity angle is strong.

blurb: Seafood, live music, local vendors, and three full days of outdoor fun — the Powder Springs Seafood Festival is a solid excuse to get outside this weekend. Thurman Springs Park is an easy drive from East Cobb, and the format is casual enough to go once or drop back in on a different day. Bring the family or make it a low-key evening out. The food is the main event, so go ready to eat.
scoring_notes: Solid community festival that fits East Cobb demographics. Free admission, outdoor, food-focused — works for families and couples alike. Less distinctive than the Greek Festival or DreamHack, but good geographic proximity and casual drop-in format work in its favor. Scoring docked on uniqueness as seafood festivals are a fairly common format.

blurb: The Marcus King Band is coming to the Fox Theatre on Saturday night, and this one is worth making the drive. Marcus King is a Grammy-nominated guitarist and singer who plays Southern rock, blues, and soul with the kind of raw energy that's hard to find in most live music right now. He's reuniting with his full band for this show, which means the sound is going to be big and the set list will dig into fan favorites alongside new material. The Fox is the Fox — there's no bad seat in the house. If you haven't seen Marcus King live yet, this is a great first time. If you have, you already know.
scoring_notes: East Cobb's educated, higher-income audience skews toward established music acts with real credibility. Marcus King hits the Southern rock and blues sweet spot that resonates with the 40+ crowd without feeling like a nostalgia act. Saturday night at the Fox is an easy sell. One-night show at an iconic venue adds urgency. Strong candidate.

event_name: Sherri Shepherd: Make It Make Sense Tour
blurb: Sherri Shepherd brings her 'Make It Make Sense' stand-up tour to the Buckhead Theatre on Saturday night, and the name alone tells you exactly what kind of show this is going to be. She's the Emmy Award-winning host of The View, and she's spent years turning the absurdity of everyday life — relationships, parenting, pop culture, all of it — into sharp, funny material that actually lands. The Buckhead Theatre is an intimate venue, which means you're close enough to feel like she's talking directly to you. This is a good night out. Grab someone worth laughing with and go.
scoring_notes: Comedy from a nationally recognized TV personality is broadly appealing to the 40+ East Cobb demographic. Buckhead Theatre is close, intimate, and easy to get to. The female-leaning appeal of The View audience is a factor — strong pick for couples or groups of women. Saturday night timing is ideal. Competes with Marcus King for the Saturday slot; Marcus King scores slightly higher on demographic breadth.

event_name: Sundays in the Park: Sounds of Spring
blurb: Sunday evening, May 17, the City of Roswell hosts 'Sounds of Spring' at Roswell City Hall as part of the free Sundays in the Park concert series. The Roswell New Horizons Pops Band — a 40-member ensemble — takes the stage for a free outdoor performance open to everyone. Bring chairs or a blanket, pack your own food and drinks, and settle in for a low-key, genuinely pleasant evening right in the neighborhood. It's the kind of thing that's easy to skip because it sounds too simple, and then you go and wonder why you don't do this every week. Free, outdoors, and close by. A good way to finish the weekend.
scoring_notes: Recurring series, which is a drawback on uniqueness. However, this is the first installment of the 2026 season, which gives it a 'kickoff' hook. Free, local, and walkable — fits the established homeowner East Cobb demographic well. More appealing to empty nesters than families with teens. Best used as a secondary pick rather than the featured event. Included here for completeness as a valid May 17 option.

blurb: If you've got a kid who plays video games — and in East Cobb, the odds are high — DreamHack Atlanta is the event they've been waiting for. It's one of the biggest gaming and esports festivals in the world, and it's coming to the Georgia World Congress Center this weekend, May 15–17. We're talking Call of Duty League Major III, Counter-Strike 2, open tournaments, indie games, cosplay, a massive expo floor, and special guests including Dwight Howard. Three-day family passes start at $99 for two adults with kids under 13 free. This is not a small lan party in someone's basement — DreamHack draws tens of thousands of people globally aka this is a legit bucket-list event for any gamer in your house. If your teenager has been asking about it, this weekend is the one.
scoring_notes: East Cobb skews families with teens and college-age kids — DreamHack targets that cohort directly. Parents with teen gamers are the likely ticket buyers, and the family pass pricing is accessible at a $118K household income. High uniqueness: this is a once-a-year major event, not a repeating local series. Adult empty nesters are less naturally drawn, but the event's scale and can't-miss factor offset that. Strong candidate for featured slot given the teen-family angle.
